002BE7r 3  A9 8A                        lda #$8A
002BE9r 3  80 08                        bra asm_common
002BEBr 3               z_asm_txa:
002BEBr 3               
002BEBr 3               xt_asm_txs:     ; txs \ TXS
002BEBr 3  A9 9A                        lda #$9A
002BEDr 3  80 04                        bra asm_common
002BEFr 3               z_asm_txs:
002BEFr 3               
002BEFr 3               xt_asm_tya:     ; tya \ TYA
002BEFr 3  A9 98                        lda #$98
002BF1r 3  80 00                        bra asm_common
002BF3r 3               z_asm_tya:
002BF3r 3               
002BF3r 3               
002BF3r 3               ; ==========================================================
002BF3r 3               ; ASSEMBLER HELPER FUNCTIONS
002BF3r 3               
002BF3r 3               asm_common:
002BF3r 3               .scope
002BF3r 3               
002BF3r 3                       ; """Common routine for all opcodes. We arrive here with the opcode in
002BF3r 3                       ; A. We do not need to check for the correct values because we are
002BF3r 3                       ; coming from the assembler Dictionary and trust our external test
002BF3r 3                       ; suite.
002BF3r 3                       ; """
002BF3r 3                               ; Compile opcode. Note cmpl_a does not use Y
002BF3r 3  A8                           tay
002BF4r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr cmpl_a
002BF7r 3               
002BF7r 3                               ; We get the length of the opcode from the table included in
002BF7r 3                               ; the disassembler. We use the opcode value as the offset in
002BF7r 3                               ; the oc_index_table. We have 256 entries, each two bytes
002BF7r 3                               ; long, so we can't just use an index with Y. We use tmp2 for
002BF7r 3                               ; this.
002BF7r 3  A9 rr                        lda #<oc_index_table
002BF9r 3  85 26                        sta tmp2
002BFBr 3  A9 rr                        lda #>oc_index_table
002BFDr 3  85 27                        sta tmp2+1
002BFFr 3               
002BFFr 3  98                           tya             ; retrieve opcode
002C00r 3  0A                           asl             ; times two for offset
002C01r 3  90 02                        bcc :+
002C03r 3  E6 27                        inc tmp2+1
002C05r 3               :
002C05r 3  A8                           tay             ; use Y as the index
002C06r 3               
002C06r 3                               ; Get address of the entry in the opcode table. We put it in
002C06r 3                               ; tmp3 and push a copy of it to the stack to be able to print
002C06r 3                               ; the opcode later
002C06r 3  B1 26                        lda (tmp2),y    ; LSB
002C08r 3  85 28                        sta tmp3
002C0Ar 3  C8                           iny
002C0Br 3  B1 26                        lda (tmp2),y    ; MSB
002C0Dr 3  85 29                        sta tmp3+1
002C0Fr 3               
002C0Fr 3  B2 28                        lda (tmp3)      ; get "lengths byte"
002C11r 3               
002C11r 3                               ; The length of the instruction is stored in bits 7 and 6.
002C11r 3                               ; Rotate them through the carry flag and mask the rest
002C11r 3  2A                           rol
002C12r 3  2A                           rol
002C13r 3  2A                           rol             ; Three times because we go through Carry
002C14r 3  29 03                        and #%00000011
002C16r 3  A8                           tay
002C17r 3               
002C17r 3                               ; One byte means no operand, we're done. Use DEY as CPY #1
002C17r 3  88                           dey
002C18r 3  F0 12                        beq _done
002C1Ar 3               
002C1Ar 3                               ; We have an operand which must be TOS
002C1Ar 3  20 rr rr                     jsr underflow_1
002C1Dr 3               
002C1Dr 3                               ; We compile the LSB of TOS as the operand we definitely have
002C1Dr 3                               ; before we even test if this is a two- or three-byte
002C1Dr 3                               ; instruction. Little endian CPU means we store this byte first
002C1Dr 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x
002C1Fr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr cmpl_a      ; does not use Y
002C22r 3               
002C22r 3                               ; If this is a two-byte instruction, we're done. If we landed
002C22r 3                               ; here, we've already decremented Y by one, so this is
002C22r 3                               ; the equivalent to CPY #2
002C22r 3  88                           dey
002C23r 3  F0 05                        beq _done_drop
002C25r 3               
002C25r 3                               ; This must be a three-byte instruction, get the MSB.
002C25r 3  B5 01                        lda 1,x
002C27r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr cmpl_a      ; Fall through to _done_drop
002C2Ar 3               
002C2Ar 3               _done_drop:
002C2Ar 3  E8                           inx
002C2Br 3  E8                           inx             ; Fall through to _done
002C2Cr 3               _done:
002C2Cr 3  60                           rts             ; Returns to original caller
002C2Dr 3               .endscope
002C2Dr 3               
002C2Dr 3               
002C2Dr 3               ; ==========================================================
002C2Dr 3               ; PSEUDO-INSTRUCTIONS AND MACROS
002C2Dr 3               
002C2Dr 3               xt_asm_push_a:
002C2Dr 3                       ; """push-a puts the content of the 65c02 Accumulator on the Forth
002C2Dr 3                       ; data stack as the TOS. This is a convience routine that encodes the
002C2Dr 3                       ; instructions  DEX  DEX  STA 0,X  STZ 1,X
002C2Dr 3                       ; """
002C2Dr 3               .scope
002C2Dr 3  A0 00                        ldy #0
002C2Fr 3               _loop:
002C2Fr 3  B9 rr rr                     lda _asmdata,y
002C32r 3  C9 FF                        cmp #$FF
002C34r 3  F0 06                        beq _done
002C36r 3               
002C36r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr cmpl_a      ; does not change Y
002C39r 3  C8                           iny
002C3Ar 3  80 F3                        bra _loop
002C3Cr 3               _done:
002C3Cr 3               .endscope
002C3Cr 3               z_asm_push_a:
002C3Cr 3  60                           rts
002C3Dr 3               _asmdata:
002C3Dr 3                       ; We can't use 00 as a terminator because STA 0,X assembles to 95 00
002C3Dr 3  CA CA 95 00          .byte $CA, $CA, $95, 00, $74, $01
002C41r 3  74 01        
002C43r 3  FF                   .byte $FF               ; terminator
002C44r 3               
002C44r 3               
002C44r 3               
002C44r 3               ; ==========================================================
002C44r 3               ; DIRECTIVES
002C44r 3               
002C44r 3               ; The "<J" directive (back jump) is a dummy instruction (syntactic sugar) to
002C44r 3               ; make clear that the JMP or JSR instructions are using the address that had
002C44r 3               ; been placed on the stack by "-->" (the "arrow" directive).
002C44r 3               xt_asm_back_jump:
002C44r 3               z_asm_back_jump:
002C44r 3  60                           rts
002C45r 3               
002C45r 3               ; The "<B" directive (back branch) takes an address that was placed on the Data
002C45r 3               ; Stack by the anonymous label directive "-->" (the "arrow") and the current
002C45r 3               ; address (via HERE) to calculate a backward branch offset. This is then stored
002C45r 3               ; by a following branch instruction.
002C45r 3               xt_asm_back_branch:
002C45r 3                               ; We arrive here with ( addr-l ) of the label on the stack and
002C45r 3                               ; then subtract the current address
002C45r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_here             ; ( addr-l addr-h )
002C48r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_minus            ; ( offset )
002C4Br 3               
002C4Br 3                               ; We subtract two more because of the branch instruction itself
002C4Br 3  3A                           dec
002C4Cr 3  3A                           dec
002C4Dr 3               
002C4Dr 3               z_asm_back_branch:
002C4Dr 3  60                           rts
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 3               assembler_end:
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 3               ; END
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 2               .include "disassembler.asm"     ; SAN disassembler
002C4Er 3               ; Disassembler for Tali Forth 2
002C4Er 3               ; Scot W. Stevenson <scot.stevenson@gmail.com>
002C4Er 3               ; First version: 28. Apr 2018
002C4Er 3               ; This version: 03. Jan 2019
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 3               ; This is the default disassembler for Tali Forth 2. Use by passing
002C4Er 3               ; the address and length of the block of memory to be disassembled:
002C4Er 3               ;
002C4Er 3               ;       disasm ( addr x -- )
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 3               ; The underflow checking is handled by the word's stub in native_words.asm, see
002C4Er 3               ; there for more information.
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 3               ; The code is disassembled in Simpler Assembler Notation (SAN), because that
002C4Er 3               ; is, uh, simpler. See the documentation and https://github.com/scotws/SAN for
002C4Er 3               ; more information. Because disassemblers are used interactively with slow
002C4Er 3               ; humans, we don't care that much about speed and put the emphasis at being
002C4Er 3               ; small.
002C4Er 3               
002C4Er 3               disassembler:
002C4Er 3               .scope
002C4Er 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_cr       ; ( addr u )
002C51r 3               _byte_loop:
002C51r 3                               ; Print address at start of the line. Note we use whatever
002C51r 3                               ; number base the user has
002C51r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_over     ; ( addr u addr )
002C54r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_u_dot    ; ( addr u )
002C57r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_space
002C5Ar 3               
002C5Ar 3                               ; We use the opcode value as the offset in the oc_index_table.
002C5Ar 3                               ; We have 256 entries, each two bytes long, so we can't just
002C5Ar 3                               ; use an index with Y. We use tmp2 for this.
002C5Ar 3  A9 rr                        lda #<oc_index_table
002C5Cr 3  85 26                        sta tmp2
002C5Er 3  A9 rr                        lda #>oc_index_table
002C60r 3  85 27                        sta tmp2+1
002C62r 3               
002C62r 3  A1 02                        lda (2,x)       ; get opcode that addr points to
002C64r 3               
002C64r 3  0A                           asl             ; multiply by two for offset
002C65r 3  90 02                        bcc :+
002C67r 3  E6 27                        inc tmp2+1      ; we're on second page
002C69r 3               :
002C69r 3  A8                           tay             ; use Y as the index
002C6Ar 3               
002C6Ar 3                               ; Get address of the entry in the opcode table. We put it
002C6Ar 3                               ; in tmp3 and push a copy of it to the stack to be able to
002C6Ar 3                               ; print the opcode later
002C6Ar 3  B1 26                        lda (tmp2),y    ; LSB
002C6Cr 3  85 28                        sta tmp3
002C6Er 3  48                           pha
002C6Fr 3               
002C6Fr 3  C8                           iny
002C70r 3               
002C70r 3  B1 26                        lda (tmp2),y    ; MSB
002C72r 3  85 29                        sta tmp3+1
002C74r 3  48                           pha
002C75r 3               
002C75r 3                               ; The first byte is the "lengths byte" which is coded so
002C75r 3                               ; that bits 7 to 6 are the length of the instruction (1 to
002C75r 3                               ; 3 bytes) and 2 to 0 are the length of the mnemonic.
002C75r 3  B2 28                        lda (tmp3)
002C77r 3  A8                           tay                     ; save copy of lengths byte
002C78r 3               
002C78r 3                               ; Since this is Simpler Assembler Notation (SAN) in a Forth
002C78r 3                               ; system, we want to print any operand before we print the
002C78r 3                               ; mnemonic ('1000 sta' instead of 'sta 1000'). This allows us
002C78r 3                               ; to copy and paste directly from the disassembler to the
002C78r 3                               ; assembler.
002C78r 3               
002C78r 3                               ; What happens next depends on the length of the instruction in
002C78r 3                               ; bytes:
002C78r 3               
002C78r 3                               ;   1 byte:  OPC          -->          OPC  bit sequence: %01
002C78r 3                               ;   2 bytes: OPC LSB      -->    0 LSB OPC  bit sequence: %10
002C78r 3                               ;   3 bytes: OPC LSB MSB  -->  MSB LSB OPC  bit sequence: %11
002C78r 3               
002C78r 3                               ; We can distinguish between the first case, where there is
002C78r 3                               ; only the mnemonic, and the second and third cases, where we
002C78r 3                               ; have an operand. We do this by use of the bit sequence in
002C78r 3                               ; bits 7 and 6.
002C78r 3  10 38                        bpl _no_operand         ; bit 7 clear, single-byte instruction
002C7Ar 3               
002C7Ar 3                               ; We have an operand. Prepare the Data Stack
002C7Ar 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; ( addr u 0 ) ZERO does not use Y
002C7Dr 3               
002C7Dr 3                               ; Because of the glory of a little endian CPU, we can start
002C7Dr 3                               ; with the next byte regardless if this is a one or two byte
002C7Dr 3                               ; operand, because we'll need the LSB one way or the other.
002C7Dr 3                               ; We have a copy of the opcode on the stack, so we can now move
002C7Dr 3                               ; to the next byte
002C7Dr 3  F6 04                        inc 4,x
002C7Fr 3  D0 02                        bne :+
002C81r 3  F6 05                        inc 5,x                 ; ( addr+1 u 0 )
002C83r 3               :
002C83r 3  B5 02                        lda 2,x
002C85r 3  D0 02                        bne :+
002C87r 3  D6 03                        dec 3,x
002C89r 3               :
002C89r 3  D6 02                        dec 2,x                 ; ( addr+1 u-1 0 )
002C8Br 3               
002C8Br 3  A1 04                        lda (4,x)
002C8Dr 3  95 00                        sta 0,x                 ; LSB of operand ( addr+1 u-1 LSB )
002C8Fr 3               
002C8Fr 3                               ; We still have a copy of the lengths byte in Y, which we use
002C8Fr 3                               ; to see if we have a one-byte operand (and are done already)
002C8Fr 3                               ; or a two-byte operand
002C8Fr 3  98                           tya                     ; retrieve copy of lengths byte
002C90r 3  2A                           rol                     ; shift bit 6 to bit 7
002C91r 3  10 12                        bpl _print_operand
002C93r 3               
002C93r 3                               ; We have a three-byte instruction, so we need to get the MSB
002C93r 3                               ; of the operand. Move to the next byte
002C93r 3  F6 04                        inc 4,x
002C95r 3  D0 02                        bne :+
002C97r 3  F6 05                        inc 5,x                 ; ( addr+2 u-1 LSB )
002C99r 3               :
002C99r 3  B5 02                        lda 2,x
002C9Br 3  D0 02                        bne :+
002C9Dr 3  D6 03                        dec 3,x
002C9Fr 3               :
002C9Fr 3  D6 02                        dec 2,x                 ; ( addr+2 u-2 LSB )
002CA1r 3               
002CA1r 3  A1 04                        lda (4,x)
002CA3r 3  95 01                        sta 1,x                 ; MSB of operand ( addr+2 u-2 opr )
002CA5r 3               
002CA5r 3                               ; fall through to _print_operand
002CA5r 3               
002CA5r 3               _print_operand:
002CA5r 3               
002CA5r 3                               ; We arrive here with the lengths byte in Y, the address of the
002CA5r 3                               ; opcode table entry for the instruction on the stack ( addr+n
002CA5r 3                               ; u-n opr). We want the output to be nicely formatted in
002CA5r 3                               ; columns, so we use U.R. The maximal width of the number in
002CA5r 3                               ; decimal on an 16-bit addressed machine is five characters
002CA5r 3  CA                           dex
002CA6r 3  CA                           dex
002CA7r 3  A9 05                        lda #5
002CA9r 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
002CABr 3  74 01                        stz 1,x                 ; ( addr+n u-n opr 5 )
002CADr 3               
002CADr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_u_dot_r          ; U.R ( addr+n u-n )
002CB0r 3               
002CB0r 3  80 0B                        bra _print_mnemonic
002CB2r 3               
002CB2r 3               _no_operand:
002CB2r 3                               ; We arrive here with the opcode table address on the stack,
002CB2r 3                               ; the lengths byte in Y and ( addr u ). Since we want to have
002CB2r 3                               ; a nicely formatted output, we need to indent the mnemonic by
002CB2r 3                               ; five spaces.
002CB2r 3  CA                           dex
002CB3r 3  CA                           dex
002CB4r 3  A9 05                        lda #5
002CB6r 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
002CB8r 3  74 01                        stz 1,x                 ; ( addr u 5 )
002CBAr 3               
002CBAr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_spaces           ; ( addr u )
002CBDr 3               
002CBDr 3                               ; fall through to _print_mnemonic
002CBDr 3               
002CBDr 3               _print_mnemonic:
002CBDr 3                               ; We arrive here with the opcode table address on the stack and
002CBDr 3                               ; ( addr u | addr+n u-n ). Time to print the mnemonic.
002CBDr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_space
002CC0r 3               
002CC0r 3  CA                           dex
002CC1r 3  CA                           dex                     ; ( addr u ? )
002CC2r 3  68                           pla                     ; MSB
002CC3r 3  95 01                        sta 1,x                 ; ( addr u MSB )
002CC5r 3  68                           pla                     ; LSB
002CC6r 3  95 00                        sta 0,x                 ; ( addr u addr-o )
002CC8r 3               
002CC8r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_count            ; ( addr u addr-o u-o )
002CCBr 3               
002CCBr 3                               ; The length of the mnemnonic string is in bits 2 to 0
002CCBr 3  74 01                        stz 1,x                 ; paranoid
002CCDr 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x
002CCFr 3  29 07                        and #%00000111          ; ( addr u addr-o u-o )
002CD1r 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
002CD3r 3               
002CD3r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_type             ; ( addr u )
002CD6r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_cr
002CD9r 3               
002CD9r 3                               ; Housekeeping: Next byte
002CD9r 3  F6 02                        inc 2,x
002CDBr 3  D0 02                        bne :+
002CDDr 3  F6 03                        inc 3,x                 ; ( addr+1 u )
002CDFr 3               :
002CDFr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_one_minus        ; ( addr+1 u-1 )
002CE2r 3               
002CE2r 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x                 ; All done?
002CE4r 3  15 01                        ora 1,x
002CE6r 3  F0 07                        beq _done
002CE8r 3               
002CE8r 3  B5 01                        lda 1,x                 ; Catch mid-instruction ranges
002CEAr 3  30 03                        bmi _done
002CECr 3               
002CECr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _byte_loop          ; out of range for BRA
002CEFr 3               _done:
002CEFr 3                               ; Clean up and leave
002CEFr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp xt_two_drop         ; JSR/RTS
002CF2r 3               .endscope
002CF2r 3               
002CF2r 3               ; =========================================================
002CF2r 3               oc_index_table:
002CF2r 3                       ; Lookup table for the instruction data (length of instruction in
002CF2r 3                       ; bytes, length of mnemonic in bytes, mnemonic string). This is used by
002CF2r 3                       ; the assembler as well.
002CF2r 3               
002CF2r 3                       ; Opcodes 00-0F
002CF2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc00, oc01, oc__, oc__, oc04, oc05, oc06, oc__
002CF6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002CFAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D02r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc08, oc09, oc0a, oc__, oc0c, oc0d, oc0e, oc0f
002D06r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D0Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D12r 3               
002D12r 3                       ; Opcodes 10-1F
002D12r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc10, oc11, oc12, oc__, oc14, oc15, oc16, oc17
002D16r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D1Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D22r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc18, oc19, oc1a, oc__, oc1c, oc1d, oc__, oc1f
002D26r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D2Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D32r 3               
002D32r 3                       ; Opcodes 20-2F
002D32r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc20, oc21, oc__, oc__, oc24, oc25, oc26, oc27
002D36r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D3Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D42r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc28, oc29, oc2a, oc__, oc2c, oc2d, oc2e, oc2f
002D46r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D4Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D52r 3               
002D52r 3                       ; Opcodes 30-3F
002D52r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc30, oc31, oc32, oc__, oc34, oc35, oc36, oc37
002D56r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D5Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D62r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc38, oc39, oc3a, oc__, oc3c, oc3d, oc3e, oc0f
002D66r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D6Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D72r 3               
002D72r 3                       ; Opcodes 40-4F
002D72r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc40, oc41, oc__, oc__, oc__, oc45, oc46, oc47
002D76r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D7Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D82r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc48, oc49, oc4a, oc__, oc4c, oc4d, oc4e, oc4f
002D86r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D8Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D92r 3               
002D92r 3                       ; Opcodes 50-5F
002D92r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc50, oc51, oc52, oc__, oc__, oc55, oc56, oc57
002D96r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002D9Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DA2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc58, oc59, oc5a, oc__, oc__, oc__, oc5e, oc5f
002DA6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DAAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DB2r 3               
002DB2r 3                       ; Opcodes 60-6F
002DB2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc60, oc61, oc__, oc__, oc64, oc65, oc66, oc67
002DB6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DBAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DC2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc68, oc69, oc6a, oc__, oc6c, oc6d, oc6e, oc6f
002DC6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DCAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DD2r 3               
002DD2r 3                       ; Opcodes 70-7F
002DD2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc70, oc71, oc72, oc__, oc74, oc75, oc76, oc77
002DD6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DDAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DE2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc78, oc79, oc7a, oc__, oc7c, oc7d, oc7e, oc7f
002DE6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DEAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DF2r 3               
002DF2r 3                       ; Opcodes 80-8F
002DF2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc80, oc81, oc__, oc__, oc84, oc85, oc86, oc__
002DF6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002DFAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E02r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc88, oc89, oc8a, oc__, oc8c, oc8d, oc8e, oc8f
002E06r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E0Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E12r 3               
002E12r 3                       ; Opcodes 90-9F
002E12r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc90, oc91, oc92, oc__, oc94, oc95, oc96, oc97
002E16r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E1Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E22r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oc98, oc99, oc9a, oc__, oc9c, oc9d, oc9e, oc9f
002E26r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E2Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E32r 3               
002E32r 3                       ; Opcodes A0-AF
002E32r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oca0, oca1, oca2, oc__, oca4, oca5, oca6, oca7
002E36r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E3Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E42r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oca8, oca9, ocaa, oc__, ocac, ocad, ocae, ocaf
002E46r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E4Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E52r 3               
002E52r 3                       ; Opcodes B0-BF
002E52r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word ocb0, ocb1, ocb2, oc__, ocb4, ocb5, ocb6, ocb7
002E56r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E5Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E62r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word ocb8, ocb9, ocba, oc__, ocbc, ocbd, ocbe, ocbf
002E66r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E6Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E72r 3               
002E72r 3                       ; Opcodes C0-CF
002E72r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word occ0, occ1, oc__, oc__, occ4, occ5, occ6, occ7
002E76r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E7Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E82r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word occ8, occ9, occa, oc__, occc, occd, occe, occf
002E86r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E8Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E92r 3               
002E92r 3                       ; Opcodes D0-DF
002E92r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word ocd0, ocd1, ocd2, oc__, oc__, ocd5, ocd6, ocd7
002E96r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002E9Ar 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EA2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word ocd8, ocd9, ocda, oc__, oc__, ocdd, ocde, ocdf
002EA6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EAAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EB2r 3               
002EB2r 3                       ; Opcodes E0-EF
002EB2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oce0, oce1, oc__, oc__, oce4, oce5, oce6, oce7
002EB6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EBAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EC2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word oce8, oce9, ocea, oc__, ocec, oced, ocee, ocef
002EC6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002ECAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002ED2r 3               
002ED2r 3                       ; Opcodes F0-FF
002ED2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word ocf0, ocf1, ocf2, oc__, oc__, ocf5, ocf6, ocf7
002ED6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EDAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EE2r 3  rr rr rr rr          .word ocf8, ocf9, ocfa, oc__, oc__, ocfd, ocfe, ocff
002EE6r 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EEAr 3  rr rr rr rr  
002EF2r 3               
002EF2r 3               
002EF2r 3               ; =========================================================
002EF2r 3               oc_table:
002EF2r 3                       ; Opcode data table for the disassember, which is also used by the
002EF2r 3                       ; assembler. Each entry starts with a "lengths byte":
002EF2r 3               
002EF2r 3                       ;       bit 7-6:  Length of instruction in bytes (1 to 3 for the 65c02)
002EF2r 3                       ;       bit 5-3:  unused
002EF2r 3                       ;       bit 2-0:  Length of mnemonic in chars (3 to 7)
002EF2r 3               
002EF2r 3                       ; To convert a line in this table to a Forth string of the mnemonic,
002EF2r 3                       ; use the COUNT word on the address of the lengths byte to get
002EF2r 3                       ; ( addr u ) and then mask all but the bits 2-0 of the TOS.
002EF2r 3               
002EF2r 3                       ; To make debugging easier, we keep the raw numbers for the lengths of
002EF2r 3                       ; the instruction and mnemonicis and let the assembler do the math
002EF2r 3                       ; required to shift and add. The actual mnemonic string follows after
002EF2r 3                       ; and is not zero terminated because we have the length in bits 2 to 0.
002EF2r 3               
002EF2r 3  83 62 72 6B  	oc00:	.byte 2*64+3, "brk"              ; enforce the signature byte
002EF6r 3  87 6F 72 61  	oc01:	.byte 2*64+7, "ora.zxi"
002EFAr 3  2E 7A 78 69  
002EFEr 3               ;      (oc02)
002EFEr 3               ;      (oc03)
002EFEr 3  85 74 73 62          oc04:   .byte 2*64+5, "tsb.z"
002F02r 3  2E 7A        
002F04r 3  85 6F 72 64  	oc05:	.byte 2*64+5, "ord.z"
002F08r 3  2E 7A        
002F0Ar 3  85 61 73 6C  	oc06:	.byte 2*64+5, "asl.z"
002F0Er 3  2E 7A        
002F10r 3               ;      (oc07)
002F10r 3  43 70 68 70  	oc08:	.byte 1*64+3, "php"
002F14r 3  85 6F 72 61  	oc09:	.byte 2*64+5, "ora.#"
002F18r 3  2E 23        
002F1Ar 3  45 61 73 6C  	oc0a:	.byte 1*64+5, "asl.a"
002F1Er 3  2E 61        
002F20r 3               ;      (oc0b)
002F20r 3  C3 74 73 62  	oc0c:	.byte 3*64+3, "tsb"
002F24r 3  C3 6F 72 61  	oc0d:	.byte 3*64+3, "ora"
002F28r 3  C3 61 73 6C  	oc0e:	.byte 3*64+3, "asl"
002F2Cr 3  C4 62 62 72  	oc0f:	.byte 3*64+4, "bbr0"
002F30r 3  30           
002F31r 3               
002F31r 3  83 62 70 6C  	oc10:	.byte 2*64+3, "bpl"
002F35r 3  87 6F 72 61  	oc11:	.byte 2*64+7, "ora.ziy"
002F39r 3  2E 7A 69 79  
002F3Dr 3  86 6F 72 61  	oc12:	.byte 2*64+6, "ora.zi"
002F41r 3  2E 7A 69

003364r 3  86 73 6D 62  	ocf7:	.byte 2*64+6, "smb7.z"
003368r 3  37 2E 7A     
00336Br 3  43 73 65 64  	ocf8:	.byte 1*64+3, "sed"
00336Fr 3  C5 73 62 63  	ocf9:	.byte 3*64+5, "sbc.y"
003373r 3  2E 79        
003375r 3  43 70 6C 78  	ocfa:	.byte 1*64+3, "plx"
003379r 3               ;      (ocfb:)
003379r 3               ;      (ocfc:)
003379r 3  C5 73 62 63  	ocfd:	.byte 3*64+5, "sbc.x"
00337Dr 3  2E 78        
00337Fr 3  C5 69 6E 63  	ocfe:	.byte 3*64+5, "inc.x"
003383r 3  2E 78        
003385r 3  C4 62 62 73  	ocff:	.byte 3*64+4, "bbs7"
003389r 3  37           
00338Ar 3               
00338Ar 3                       ; Common routine for opcodes that are not supported by the 65c02
00338Ar 3  01 3F        	oc__:	.byte 1, "?"
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; used to calculate size of assembled disassembler code
00338Cr 3               disassembler_end:
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 2               .include "ed.asm"               ; Line-based editor ed6502
00338Cr 3               ; ed6502 - Ed-like line-based editor for Tali Forth 2
00338Cr 3               ; Scot W. Stevenson <scot.stevenson@gmail.com>
00338Cr 3               ; First version: 13. Okt 2018
00338Cr 3               ; This version: 28. Dec 2018
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; Ed is a line-orientated editor for Tali Forth 2 based on the classic Unix
00338Cr 3               ; editor of the same name. It is included because a) I like line editors and
00338Cr 3               ; this is my project, so there, and b) as a very simple editor that will work
00338Cr 3               ; even if there is no vt100 terminal support, just with ASCII if needs be. For
00338Cr 3               ; further information on ed, see
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ;   https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ed_(text_editor)
00338Cr 3               ;   https://www.gnu.org/software/ed/ed.html
00338Cr 3               ;   https://www.gnu.org/software/ed/manual/ed_manual.html
00338Cr 3               ;   https://sanctum.geek.nz/arabesque/actually-using-ed/
00338Cr 3               ;   http://www.psue.uni-hannover.de/wise2017_2018/material/ed.pdf
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; We start editor from Forth with
00338Cr 3               ;
00338Cr 3               ;       ed ( -- addr u )
00338Cr 3               ;
00338Cr 3               ; The return values ( addr u ) are the address and length of the text written.
00338Cr 3               ; If no text was written, u is zero and addr is undefined.
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; In the working memory, the text is stored as a simple linked list of lines.
00338Cr 3               ; Each node consists of three 16-bit entries:
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ;       - pointer to next entry (0 for end of list)
00338Cr 3               ;       - pointer to beginning of string ( addr )
00338Cr 3               ;       - length of string ( u )
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; The editor only works in interaction with slow humans, so speed is not
00338Cr 3               ; a primary concern. We try to keep the size down instead.
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; Where to put variables is a bit of a problem. To convert the numbers, we need
00338Cr 3               ; UM/MOD, which uses the scratchpad, and ACCEPT uses tmp1, tmp2, and tmp3 at
00338Cr 3               ; some point, so we either have to pay very close attention, or we do something
00338Cr 3               ; else. After some experimenting, it seems that the easiest way for this sort
00338Cr 3               ; of hybrid Forth/assembler system is to keep the parameters for the commands
00338Cr 3               ; on the Data Stack in the form of ( para1 para2 ):
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ;       TOS: parameter 2 (after the comma)
00338Cr 3               ;       NOS: parameter 1 (before the comma)
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; The third and fourth entries on the stack are the ( addr-t u-t ) entries the
00338Cr 3               ; text will be/has been written to, or u as 0 if nothing was defined.
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ; We also need a pointer to the beginning of the text (first node of the list),
00338Cr 3               ; the number of the current line, and a flag to mark if the text has been
00338Cr 3               ; changed. We have six bytes of zero page reserved for any editor to use. Note
00338Cr 3               ; that this means that we can't use two editors at the same time, which won't
00338Cr 3               ; be a problem until we can multitask.
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ed_head  := editor1  ; pointer to first list element (addr) (2 bytes)
00338Cr 3               ed_cur   := editor2  ; current line number (1 is first line) (2 bytes)
00338Cr 3               ed_flags := editor3  ; Flags used by ed, where
00338Cr 3               ;       bit 7 parameters - 0: none, 1: have at least one parameter
00338Cr 3               ;       bit 6 changed    - 0: text not changed, 1: text was changed
00338Cr 3               ;       bit 0 printing   - 0: no line numbers (p), 1: with line numbers (n)
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ;  Byte editor3+1 is currently unused
00338Cr 3               
00338Cr 3               ed6502:
00338Cr 3               .scope
00338Cr 3                               ; Start a new empty linked list at HERE. This is also
00338Cr 3                               ; the current line
00338Cr 3  64 2E                        stz ed_head
00338Er 3  64 2F                        stz ed_head+1
003390r 3               
003390r 3                               ; The current line is 0, because we start counting at
003390r 3                               ; line 1 for the humans
003390r 3  64 30                        stz ed_cur
003392r 3  64 31                        stz ed_cur+1
003394r 3               
003394r 3                               ; At the beginning, we have no parameters (bit 7), no line
003394r 3                               ; numbers (bit 0), and nothing was changed (bit 6)
003394r 3  64 32                        stz ed_flags
003396r 3               
003396r 3                               ; We put zeros as placeholders for the text we've written to
003396r 3                               ; (the "target") on the stack. Because the stack picture is
003396r 3                               ; going to get very confusing very fast, we'll mark them
003396r 3                               ; specially with "-t" suffixes in the stack comments.
003396r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero
003399r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; ( addr-t u-t )
00339Cr 3               
00339Cr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_cr
00339Fr 3               
00339Fr 3               _input_loop:
00339Fr 3                               ; Set parameter flag to none (bit 7); default printing is
00339Fr 3                               ; without line numbers (bit 0). We leave the changed flag (bit
00339Fr 3                               ; 6) because we might be coming from a previous add
00339Fr 3  A9 81                        lda #%10000001
0033A1r 3  14 32                        trb ed_flags
0033A3r 3               
0033A3r 3                               ; We really don't want to have to write a complete
0033A3r 3                               ; parser for such a simple editor, so we walk through the
0033A3r 3                               ; possibilities the hard way. Get input from the user. This
0033A3r 3                               ; routine handles any errors from REFILL
0033A3r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _get_input
0033A6r 3               
0033A6r 3                               ; If we were not given an empty line, see what we were given
0033A6r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
0033A8r 3  D0 1F                        bne _command_mode
0033AAr 3               
0033AAr 3                               ; We were given an empty line. Advance one line, print it, and
0033AAr 3                               ; make it the new current line
0033AAr 3  CA                           dex
0033ABr 3  CA                           dex                     ; ( addr-t u-t ? )
0033ACr 3               
0033ACr 3  A5 30                        lda ed_cur
0033AEr 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
0033B0r 3  A5 31                        lda ed_cur+1
0033B2r 3  95 01                        sta 1,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t u )
0033B4r 3               
0033B4r 3                               ; This counts as having a parameter
0033B4r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
0033B6r 3  04 32                        tsb ed_flags
0033B8r 3               
0033B8r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_one_plus         ; ( addr-t u-t u+1 )
0033BBr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _is_valid_line
0033BEr 3  B0 03                        bcs :+
0033C0r 3               
0033C0r 3                               ; New line number is not legal, abort
0033C0r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _error_1drop
0033C3r 3               :
0033C3r 3                               ; We have a legal line number, but we need two entries on
0033C3r 3                               ; the parameter list (four if you count the target
0033C3r 3                               ; address) to be able to work with the rest of the program.
0033C3r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; ( addr-t u-t u+1 0 )
0033C6r 3               
0033C6r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _line_number_only_from_external
0033C9r 3               
0033C9r 3               _command_mode:
0033C9r 3               
0033C9r 3                               ; We were given something other than an empty line. Set the
0033C9r 3                               ; parameter variables to zero as the default. There is no line
0033C9r 3                               ; zero, because we're coding for normal, sane humans, not weird
0033C9r 3                               ; computer people. Some commands like "a" will take a "line 0",
0033C9r 3                               ; however. We use the ed_flags bit 7 to signal if we are
0033C9r 3                               ; without parameters.
0033C9r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; parameter 1 is NOS ( addr-t u-t 0 )
0033CCr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; parameter 2 is TOS ( addr-t u-t 0 0 )
0033CFr 3               
0033CFr 3                               ; We start off by taking care of any parameters. These can be
0033CFr 3                               ; '%' for the complete text, '$' for the last line, a line
0033CFr 3                               ; number, or a line number followed by a ',' and then either
0033CFr 3                               ; the '$' for the last line or another number. (The original
0033CFr 3                               ; Unix ed has more options, but we're ignoring them for the
0033CFr 3                               ; moment.) In pseudocode, what we are doing in this stage looks
0033CFr 3                               ; something like this:
0033CFr 3               
0033CFr 3                               ;        case char = '.':
0033CFr 3                               ;              para1 = current line
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;        case char = '$':
0033CFr 3                               ;              para1 = last line
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;        case char = '%' or ',':
0033CFr 3                               ;              para1 = 1
0033CFr 3                               ;              para2 = last line
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;        case char = ';':
0033CFr 3                               ;              para1 = current line
0033CFr 3                               ;              para2 = last line
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;        case number:
0033CFr 3                               ;              para1 = number
0033CFr 3                               ;              get next char
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;              if char = ',':
0033CFr 3                               ;                      get next char
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;                      case char = '$':
0033CFr 3                               ;                              para2 = last line
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;                      case number:
0033CFr 3                               ;                              para2 = number
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;                      else error
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;              else get previous char
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;        else error
0033CFr 3                               ;
0033CFr 3                               ;        get next char
0033CFr 3                               ;        process command char
0033CFr 3               
0033CFr 3                               ; We use the Y register as an offset to the beginning of the
0033CFr 3                               ; character input buffer (cib) because we're never going to
0033CFr 3                               ; have more than 255 characters of input with ed and we don't
0033CFr 3                               ; want to have to duplicate the complete machinery required for
0033CFr 3                               ; >IN. In other words, >IN has no meaning for ed. This means
0033CFr 3                               ; that every jmp to _check_command must have Y in a defined
0033CFr 3                               ; state, which is different from the rest of Tali Forth.
0033CFr 3               
0033CFr 3                               ; Parameter processing could probably be handled more
0033CFr 3                               ; efficiently with a loop construct similar to the way the
0033CFr 3                               ; commands are taken care of below. We'll revisit this once ed
0033CFr 3                               ; is feature complete, because of the evils of premature
0033CFr 3                               ; optimization.
0033CFr 3               
0033CFr 3               _prefix_dot:
0033CFr 3                               ; --- . --- Designate current line for further operations
0033CFr 3  B2 08                        lda (cib)
0033D1r 3  C9 2E                        cmp #$2e                ; ASCII '.'
0033D3r 3  D0 3A                        bne _prefix_dollar
0033D5r 3               
0033D5r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _have_text
0033D8r 3               
0033D8r 3  A5 30                        lda ed_cur
0033DAr 3  95 02                        sta 2,x
0033DCr 3  A5 31                        lda ed_cur+1
0033DEr 3  95 03                        sta 3,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t cur 0 )
0033E0r 3               
0033E0r 3                               ; We have a parameter
0033E0r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
0033E2r 3  04 32                        tsb ed_flags
0033E4r 3               
0033E4r 3                               ; If we were only given a '.', we print the current line and are
0033E4r 3                               ; done
0033E4r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
0033E6r 3  3A                           dec                     ; sets Z if A was 1
0033E7r 3  D0 03                        bne :+
0033E9r 3               
0033E9r 3                               ; We know that we have some text and the number of the last
0033E9r 3                               ; line was provided by _last_line, so in theory we don't have
0033E9r 3                               ; to check if this is a legal line number. However, we keep one
0033E9r 3                               ; entry point, so the check is repeated further down. Call it
0033E9r 3                               ; paranoia.
0033E9r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _line_number_only_from_external
0033ECr 3               :
0033ECr 3                               ; We have processed the first parameter, and know that we have
0033ECr 3                               ; more than just a dot here. We now need to see if the next
0033ECr 3                               ; character is a comma or a command character. To do this, we
0033ECr 3                               ; need to modify the stack to ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr u )
0033ECr 3  CA                           dex
0033EDr 3  CA                           dex
0033EEr 3  CA                           dex
0033EFr 3  CA                           dex
0033F0r 3               
0033F0r 3  A5 08                        lda cib
0033F2r 3  95 02                        sta 2,x
0033F4r 3  A5 09                        lda cib+1
0033F6r 3  95 03                        sta 3,x
0033F8r 3               
0033F8r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
0033FAr 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
0033FCr 3  A5 0B                        lda ciblen+1
0033FEr 3  95 01                        sta 1,x
003400r 3               
003400r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_one_minus        ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr u-1 )
003403r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_swap             ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 u-1 addr )
003406r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_one_plus         ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 u-1 addr+1 )
003409r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_swap             ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr+1 u-1 )
00340Cr 3               
00340Cr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _check_for_para2
00340Fr 3               
00340Fr 3               _prefix_dollar:
00340Fr 3                               ; --- $ --- Designate last line for further operations
00340Fr 3  B2 08                        lda (cib)
003411r 3  C9 24                        cmp #'$'
003413r 3  D0 1C                        bne _prefix_percent
003415r 3               
003415r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _have_text
003418r 3               
003418r 3  E8                           inx
003419r 3  E8                           inx                     ; ( addr-t u-t 0 )
00341Ar 3               
00341Ar 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _last_line          ; ( addr-t u-t 0 para1 )
00341Dr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_swap             ; SWAP ( addr-t u-t para1 0 )
003420r 3               
003420r 3                               ; We have a parameter
003420r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
003422r 3  04 32                        tsb ed_flags
003424r 3               
003424r 3                               ; If we were only given a '$', we print the last line and are
003424r 3                               ; done
003424r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
003426r 3  3A                           dec                     ; sets Z if A was 1
003427r 3  D0 03                        bne :+
003429r 3               
003429r 3                               ; We know that we have some text and the number of the last
003429r 3                               ; line was provided by _last_line, so in theory we don't have
003429r 3                               ; to check if this is a legal line number. However, we keep one
003429r 3                               ; entry point for the moment and repeat the check further down
003429r 3                               ; out of paranoia
003429r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _line_number_only_from_external
00342Cr 3               :
00342Cr 3                               ; We are one character into the input buffer cib, so we advance
00342Cr 3                               ; Y as the index accordingly
00342Cr 3  A0 01                        ldy #01
00342Er 3               
00342Er 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _check_command
003431r 3               
003431r 3               _prefix_percent:
003431r 3                               ; --- % and , --- Designate whole text for futher operations
003431r 3  B2 08                        lda (cib)
003433r 3  C9 25                        cmp #$25                ; ASCII '%'
003435r 3  F0 04                        beq _whole_text
003437r 3  C9 2C                        cmp #$2c                ; ASCII ','
003439r 3  D0 17                        bne _prefix_semicolon
00343Br 3               
00343Br 3               _whole_text:
00343Br 3                               ; If there is no text yet, print an error
00343Br 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _have_text
00343Er 3               
00343Er 3                               ; We have at least one line of text. The first parameter
00343Er 3                               ; is therefore line one, the second the last line
00343Er 3  A9 01                        lda #01
003440r 3  95 02                        sta 2,x                 ; LSB of NOS is para 1
003442r 3  74 03                        stz 3,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 )
003444r 3               
003444r 3               _semicolon_entry:
003444r 3                               ; Get the number (not the address) of the last line and
003444r 3                               ; store it as the second parameter
003444r 3  E8                           inx
003445r 3  E8                           inx                     ; DROP ( addr-t u-t para1 )
003446r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _last_line          ; ( addr-t u-t para1 para2 )
003449r 3               
003449r 3                               ; We have a parameter
003449r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
00344Br 3  04 32                        tsb ed_flags
00344Dr 3               
00344Dr 3                               ; We are one character into the input buffer cib, so we advance
00344Dr 3                               ; Y as the index accordingly
00344Dr 3  A0 01                        ldy #01
00344Fr 3               
00344Fr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _check_command
003452r 3               
003452r 3               _prefix_semicolon:
003452r 3                               ; --- ; --- Designate from current line to end of text
003452r 3  B2 08                        lda (cib)
003454r 3  C9 3B                        cmp #$3b                ; ASCII ';'
003456r 3  D0 0D                        bne _prefix_number
003458r 3               
003458r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _have_text
00345Br 3               
00345Br 3                               ; The first parameter is the current line
00345Br 3  A5 30                        lda ed_cur
00345Dr 3  95 02                        sta 2,x
00345Fr 3  A5 31                        lda ed_cur+1
003461r 3  95 03                        sta 3,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t cur 0 )
003463r 3               
003463r 3                               ; The second parameter is the last line. We've done this part
003463r 3                               ; before for the '%' and ',' parameters, so we reuse that code
003463r 3  80 DF                        bra _semicolon_entry
003465r 3               
003465r 3               _prefix_number:
003465r 3                               ; --- <NUM> --- Check if we have been given a number
003465r 3               
003465r 3                               ; We use the built-in Forth routines for this, which involves
003465r 3                               ; calling >NUMBER, which calls UM*, which uses tmp1, tmp2, and
003465r 3                               ; tmp3. So we can't use any of those temporary variables. We
003465r 3                               ; arrive here with ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ), which doesn't help us at
003465r 3                               ; all because the string we are looking at is in ( cib ciblen )
003465r 3               
003465r 3                               ; Set up >NUMBER using CIB and CIBLEN as the location of the
003465r 3                               ; string to check. First, though, add the "accumulator" of
003465r 3                               ; >NUMBER as a double number, that is, to single-cell numbers
003465r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero
003468r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 0 0 )
00346Br 3               
00346Br 3  CA                           dex
00346Cr 3  CA                           dex
00346Dr 3  CA                           dex
00346Er 3  CA                           dex                     ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 0 0 ? ? )
00346Fr 3               
00346Fr 3  A5 08                        lda cib
003471r 3  95 02                        sta 2,x
003473r 3  A5 09                        lda cib+1
003475r 3  95 03                        sta 3,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 0 0 cib ? )
003477r 3               
003477r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
003479r 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
00347Br 3  A5 0B                        lda ciblen+1
00347Dr 3  95 01                        sta 1,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 0 0 cib ciblen )
00347Fr 3               
00347Fr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_to_number        ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud addr2 u2 )
003482r 3               
003482r 3                               ; If we converted all the characters in the string (u2 is
003482r 3                               ; zero), then the user just gave us a line number to
003482r 3                               ; jump to and nothing else. Otherwise, take another look
003482r 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x
003484r 3  15 01                        ora 1,x
003486r 3  D0 24                        bne _have_unconverted_chars
003488r 3               
003488r 3                               ; We must have a line number and nothing else. Make this
003488r 3                               ; the current line number and print the line. Remember
003488r 3                               ; that at this point, the line number still could be a zero
003488r 3  E8                           inx
003489r 3  E8                           inx
00348Ar 3  E8                           inx
00348Br 3  E8                           inx                     ; 2DROP ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud )
00348Cr 3               
00348Cr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_d_to_s           ; D>S ( addr-t u-t 0 0 u )
00348Fr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_not_rote         ; -ROT ( addr-t u-t u 0 0 )
003492r 3               
003492r 3  E8                           inx
003493r 3  E8                           inx                     ; ( addr-t u-t u 0 ) drop through
003494r 3               
003494r 3               _line_number_only_from_external:
003494r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_swap             ; ( addr-t u-t 0 u )
003497r 3               
003497r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _is_valid_line
00349Ar 3  B0 03                        bcs :+
00349Cr 3               
00349Cr 3                               ; This is not a valid line number, so we bail
00349Cr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _error_2drop
00349Fr 3               :
00349Fr 3                               ; Legal line number, so make it the current number
00349Fr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_swap             ; ( addr-t u-t u 0 )
0034A2r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _para1_to_cur
0034A5r 3               
0034A5r 3                               ; We have a parameter
0034A5r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
0034A7r 3  04 32                        tsb ed_flags
0034A9r 3               
0034A9r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _cmd_p_from_external
0034ACr 3               
0034ACr 3               _have_unconverted_chars:
0034ACr 3                               ; We have some unconverted characters left. If none of the
0034ACr 3                               ; characters were converted, we probably just got a
0034ACr 3                               ; command character and need to skip the rest of the prefix
0034ACr 3                               ; processing. In this case, the number of unconverted
0034ACr 3                               ; characters is equal to the length of the string.
0034ACr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_dup              ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud addr2 u2 u2 )
0034AFr 3               
0034AFr 3  CA                           dex
0034B0r 3  CA                           dex                     ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud addr2 u2 u2 ? )
0034B1r 3               
0034B1r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
0034B3r 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
0034B5r 3  A5 0B                        lda ciblen+1
0034B7r 3  95 01                        sta 1,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud addr2 u2 u2 ciblen )
0034B9r 3               
0034B9r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_equal            ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud addr2 u2 f )
0034BCr 3               
0034BCr 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x
0034BEr 3  15 01                        ora 1,x
0034C0r 3  F0 0E                        beq _no_command_yet
0034C2r 3               
0034C2r 3                               ; The length of the input string is equal to the length of the
0034C2r 3                               ; unprocessed string that >NUMBER returned. Put differently,
0034C2r 3                               ; the first character isn't a number. We know that it isn't '$'
0034C2r 3                               ; or '%' either, so we assume that it's a command character.
0034C2r 3               
0034C2r 3                               ; Clear up the stack and process that command character
0034C2r 3  8A                           txa
0034C3r 3  18                           clc
0034C4r 3  69 0A                        adc #10
0034C6r 3  AA                           tax                     ; ( addr-t u-t 0 0 )
0034C7r 3               
0034C7r 3                               ; If we weren't given a number, this means we didn't explicitly
0034C7r 3                               ; get a 0 either. So we don't have a parameter. This is the
0034C7r 3                               ; default case, but out of paranoia we explicity clear the flag
0034C7r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
0034C9r 3  14 32                        trb ed_flags
0034CBr 3               
0034CBr 3                               ; We don't have any offset, so we go with Y as zero
0034CBr 3  A0 00                        ldy #00
0034CDr 3               
0034CDr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _check_command
0034D0r 3               
0034D0r 3               _no_command_yet:
0034D0r 3                               ; There actually seems to be a parameter number present.
0034D0r 3                               ; Save the number we converted as the first parameter. We
0034D0r 3                               ; arrive here with ( addr-t u-t 0 0 ud addr2 u2 f ) from
0034D0r 3                               ; >NUMBER. To avoid too long stack comments, we leave the
0034D0r 3                               ; target addresses out in this next code segment.
0034D0r 3  E8                           inx
0034D1r 3  E8                           inx                     ; ( ... 0 0 ud addr2 u2 )
0034D2r 3               
0034D2r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_to_r             ; >R ( ... 0 0 ud addr2 ) (R: u2)
0034D5r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_not_rote         ; -ROT ( ... 0 0 addr2 ud ) (R: u2)
0034D8r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_d_to_s           ; D>S  ( ... 0 0 addr2 para1 ) (R: u2)
0034DBr 3               
0034DBr 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x                 ; LSB
0034DDr 3  95 06                        sta 6,x
0034DFr 3  B5 01                        lda 1,x                 ; MSB
0034E1r 3  95 07                        sta 7,x                 ; ( ... para1 0 addr2 para1 ) (R: u2)
0034E3r 3               
0034E3r 3  E8                           inx
0034E4r 3  E8                           inx                     ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr2 ) (R: u2)
0034E5r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_r_from           ; R> ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr2 u2 ) fall through
0034E8r 3               
0034E8r 3                               ; We have a parameter
0034E8r 3  A9 80                        lda #%10000000
0034EAr 3  04 32                        tsb ed_flags
0034ECr 3               
0034ECr 3               _check_for_para2:
0034ECr 3                               ; That was the first parameter. If the next character is
0034ECr 3                               ; a comma, then there is a second parameter (another number
0034ECr 3                               ; or '$'). Otherwise we expect a command. This is the entry
0034ECr 3                               ; point if the first character was a dot (eg '.,3p')
0034ECr 3  A1 02                        lda (2,x)
0034EEr 3               
0034EEr 3  C9 2C                        cmp #$2c                ; ASCII code for ',' (comma)
0034F0r 3  F0 0D                        beq _got_comma
0034F2r 3               
0034F2r 3                               ; It's not a comma, so it's going to be a command character.
0034F2r 3                               ; We need to figure out how many digits our number has so
0034F2r 3                               ; we can adjust Y as the offset. We don't have to do this with
0034F2r 3                               ; 16 bit because no input string is going to be that long
0034F2r 3  38                           sec
0034F3r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
0034F5r 3  F5 00                        sbc 0,x
0034F7r 3  A8                           tay
0034F8r 3               
0034F8r 3                               ; Remove the leftover stuff from >NUMBER
0034F8r 3  E8                           inx
0034F9r 3  E8                           inx
0034FAr 3  E8                           inx
0034FBr 3  E8                           inx                     ; 2DROP ( addr-t u-t para1 0 )
0034FCr 3               
0034FCr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _check_command
0034FFr 3               
0034FFr 3               _got_comma:
0034FFr 3                               ; It's a comma, so we have a second parameter. The next
0034FFr 3                               ; character can either be '$' to signal the end of the text
0034FFr 3                               ; or another number. First, though, move to that next char
0034FFr 3  F6 02                        inc 2,x
003501r 3  D0 02                        bne :+
003503r 3  F6 03                        inc 3,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr2+1 u2 )
003505r 3               :
003505r 3  B5 01                        lda 1,x
003507r 3  F0 02                        beq :+
003509r 3  D6 01                        dec 1,x
00350Br 3               :
00350Br 3  D6 00                        dec 0,x                 ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 addr2+1 u2-1 )
00350Dr 3               
00350Dr 3                               ; See if this is an end-of-line '$'
00350Dr 3  A1 02                        lda (2,x)
00350Fr 3  C9 24                        cmp #$24                ; ASCII for '$'
003511r 3  D0 14                        bne _para2_not_dollar
003513r 3               
003513r 3                               ; It's a dollar sign, which means para2 is the number of the
003513r 3                               ; last line of the text. We need to adjust Y as the offset. We
003513r 3                               ; assume that no command line will be longer than 255
003513r 3                               ; characters in ed so we can get away with just looking at
003513r 3                               ; the LSB
003513r 3  38                           sec
003514r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
003516r 3  F5 02                        sbc 2,x
003518r 3  A8                           tay
003519r 3               
003519r 3                               ; However, we need to move Y up by one because we were on the
003519r 3                               ; '$' and not on the character after that
003519r 3  C8                           iny
00351Ar 3  5A                           phy
00351Br 3               
00351Br 3                               ; Dump all the stuff from >NUMBER off the stack. This saves
00351Br 3                               ; one byte compared to six INX instructions, and a byte saved
00351Br 3                               ; is a byte earned.
00351Br 3  8A                           txa
00351Cr 3  18                           clc
00351Dr 3  69 06                        adc #06
00351Fr 3  AA                           tax                     ; ( addr-t u-t para1 )
003520r 3               
003520r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _last_line          ; ( addr-t u-t para1 para2 )
003523r 3               
003523r 3  7A                           ply
003524r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _check_command
003527r 3               
003527r 3               _para2_not_dollar:
003527r 3                               ; It's not a dollar sign, so it is either another number or an
003527r 3                               ; error. We try for a number first. We arrive here with ( para1
003527r 3                               ; 0 addr2+1 u2-1 ), which u2-1 pointing to the first mystery
003527r 3                               ; character after the comma. Again, we skip the ( addr-t u-t )
003527r 3                               ; at the beginning of the stack comment here.
003527r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_to_r             ; >R ( ... para1 0 addr2+1 ) (R: u2-1)
00352Ar 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; 0 ( ... para1 0 addr2+1 0 ) (R: u2-1)
00352Dr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_zero             ; 0 ( ... para1 0 addr2+1 0 0 ) (R: u2-1)
003530r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_rot              ; ROT ( ... para1 0 0 0 addr2+1 ) (R: u2-1)
003533r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_r_from           ; R> ( ... para1 0 0 0 addr2+1 u2-1)
003536r 3               
003536r 3                               ; We'll need a copy of the length of the rest of the string to
003536r 3                               ; see if we've actually done any work
003536r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_dup              ; DUP ( ... para1 0 0 0 addr2+1 u2-1 u2-1)
003539r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_to_r             ; >R ( ... para1 0 0 0 addr2+1 u2-1 ) (R: u2-1)
00353Cr 3               
00353Cr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_to_number        ; >NUMBER ( ... para1 0 ud addr3 u3 ) (R: u2-1)
00353Fr 3               
00353Fr 3                               ; If the original string and the leftover string have the same
00353Fr 3                               ; length, then nothing was converted and we have an error
00353Fr 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_dup              ; DUP ( ... para1 0 ud addr3 u3 u3 ) (R: u2-1)
003542r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_r_from           ; R> ( ... para1 0 ud addr3 u3 u3 u2-1 )
003545r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_equal            ; = ( ... para1 0 ud addr3 u3 f )
003548r 3               
003548r 3  B5 00                        lda 0,x
00354Ar 3  15 01                        ora 1,x
00354Cr 3  F0 08                        beq _second_number
00354Er 3               
00354Er 3                               ; The strings are the same length, so nothing was converted, so
00354Er 3                               ; we have an error. We have to get all that stuff off the
00354Er 3                               ; stack first
00354Er 3  8A                           txa
00354Fr 3  18                           clc
003550r 3  69 0C                        adc #12
003552r 3  AA                           tax                     ; back to ( addr-t u-t )
003553r 3               
003553r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _error
003556r 3               
003556r 3               _second_number:
003556r 3                               ; We have a second number, so we add it to para2. We arrive here
003556r 3                               ; with ( para1 0 ud addr3 u3 f )
003556r 3  E8                           inx
003557r 3  E8                           inx                     ; ( addr-t u-t para1 0 ud addr3 u3 )
003558r 3               
003558r 3                               ; Calculate the offset for Y
003558r 3  38                           sec
003559r 3  A5 0A                        lda ciblen
00355Br 3  F5 00                        sbc 0,x
00355Dr 3  48                           pha
00355Er 3               
00355Er 3                               ; Clean up the stack
00355Er 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_two_drop         ; 2DROP ( addr-t u-t para1 0 ud )
003561r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_d_to_s           ; D>S  ( addr-t u-t para1 0 para2 )
003564r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_nip              ; NIP ( addr-t u-t para1 para2 )
003567r 3               
003567r 3  7A                           ply
003568r 3               
003568r 3                               ; fall through to _check_command
003568r 3               
003568r 3               _check_command:
003568r 3                               ; At this point, we assume that we have handled any parameters
003568r 3                               ; which are now in their place on the stack, which must have
003568r 3                               ; the format ( addr-t u-t para1 para2 ). Also, any offset to CIB
003568r 3                               ; is going to be in Y. Bit 7 in ed_flags signals if we have
003568r 3                               ; a parameter or not.
003568r 3               
003568r 3                               ; Command character checking works by comparing the char we
003568r 3                               ; have at CIB+Y with a list of legal characters. The index in
003568r 3                               ; the list is the index of the command's routine in a jump
003568r 3                               ; table. The list itself is zero-terminated, which is okay
003568r 3                               ; because we've taken care of any legal parameters.
003568r 3  B1 08                        lda (cib),y             ; get mystery char from input
00356Ar 3  85 24                        sta tmp1
00356Cr 3               
00356Cr 3                               ; We're going to need X for the jump table, so it has to
00356Cr 3                               ; take a break from being the Data Stack Pointer (DSP). Pushing
00356Cr 3                               ; X to the stack uses less space than storing in the reserved
00356Cr 3                               ; space on the Zero Page
00356Cr 3  DA                           phx
00356Dr 3  A2 00                        ldx #00
00356Fr 3               _cmd_loop:
00356Fr 3  BD rr rr                     lda ed_cmd_list,x
003572r 3  F0 07                        beq _illegal_command    ; zero marks end of list
003574r 3               
003574r 3  C5 24                        cmp tmp1
003576r 3  F0 07                        beq _found_cmd
003578r 3               
003578r 3                               ; No match, next char
003578r 3  E8                           inx
003579r 3  80 F4                        bra _cmd_loop
00357Br 3               
00357Br 3               _illegal_command:
00357Br 3                               ; Whatever the user gave us, we don't recognize it
00357Br 3  FA                           plx
00357Cr 3               
00357Cr 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _error_2drop
00357Fr 3               
00357Fr 3               _found_cmd:
00357Fr 3                               ; We have a command match. Because this is the 65c02 and not
00357Fr 3                               ; the 65816, we can only use JMP (addr,x) and not a subroutine
00357Fr 3                               ; jump. That sucks.
00357Fr 3  8A                           txa
003580r 3  0A                           asl
003581r 3  AA                           tax                     ; X * 2 for table
003582r 3               
003582r 3                               ; Note we're jumping with the DSP still on the stack, so each
003582r 3                               ; command routine has to pull it into X the very first thing
003582r 3                               ; with its very own PLX. There doesn't seem to be a sane way to
003582r 3                               ; avoid this.
003582r 3  7C rr rr                     jmp (ed_cmd_table,x)
003585r 3               
003585r 3               _next_command:
003585r 3                               ; Clean up the stack and return to the input loop. We
003585r 3                               ; arrive here with ( addr-t u-t para1 para2 ). The called
003585r 3                               ; command routines have taken care of putting the DSP (that's
003585r 3                               ; X) back the way it should be
003585r 3  E8                           inx
003586r 3  E8                           inx
003587r 3  E8                           inx
003588r 3  E8                           inx                     ; ( addr-t u-t ) Fall through
003589r 3               
003589r 3               _next_command_empty:
003589r 3                               ; The beginning of the input loop takes care of resetting the
003589r 3                               ; parameter flag
003589r 3  4C rr rr                     jmp _input_loop
00358Cr 3               
00358Cr 3               _all_done:
00358Cr 3                               ; That's enough for ed today. We have to clear out the input
00358Cr 3                               ; buffer or else the Forth main main loop will react to the
00358Cr 3                               ; last input command
00358Cr 3  64 0A                        stz ciblen
00358Er 3  64 0B                        stz ciblen+1
003590r 3               
003590r 3                               ; Clean up the stack
003590r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_two_drop                 ; 2DROP ( addr-t u-t )
003593r 3               
003593r 3  60                           rts
003594r 3               
003594r 3               
003594r 3               ; === COMMAND ROUTINES ====
003594r 3               
003594r 3               ; We enter all command subroutines with ( addr-t u-t para1 para2 ) and the DSP
003594r 3               ; still on the Return Stack. This means that the first oder of business is to
003594r 3               ; restore the DSP with PLX -- remember this when you add new commands. At this
003594r 3               ; point, we don't need the offset in Y anymore so we are free to use it as we
003594r 3               ; please.
003594r 3               
003594r 3               ; There is potential to rewrite many of the command routines with an abstract
003594r 3               ; construct in the form of (pseudocode):
003594r 3               
003594r 3               ;       f = cmd         ; command such as d, p, n, as a function
003594r 3               ;       map f range(para1, para2)
003594r 3               
003594r 3               ; That is, have one routine with a looping structure and pass the actual work
003594r 3               ; as a function. However, this is 8-bit assembler and not, say, Haskell, so
003594r 3               ; that abstraction will wait for a future round of refracturing when we have
003594r 3               ; everything complete and working.
003594r 3               
003594r 3               ; -------------------------
003594r 3               _cmd_a:
003594r 3                       ; a -- Add text after current/given line. If no line is given, we use
003594r 3                       ; the current line. We accept the number '0' and then start adding at
003594r 3                       ; the very beginning. The second parameter is always ignored. This
003594r 3                       ; routine is used by i as well.
003594r 3  FA                           plx
003595r 3               
003595r 3                               ; We don't care about para2, because a just adds stuff starting
003595r 3                               ; the line we were given
003595r 3  E8                           inx
003596r 3  E8                           inx                     ;  DROP ( addr-t u-t para1 )
003597r 3               
003597r 3                               ; If we weren't given a parameter, make the current line the
003597r 3                               ; parameter
003597r 3  24 32                        bit ed_flags
003599r 3  30 08                        bmi _cmd_a_have_para
00359Br 3               
00359Br 3  A5 30                        lda ed_cur
00359Dr 3  95 00                        sta 0,x
00359Fr 3  A5 31                        lda ed_cur+1
0035A1r 3  95 01                        sta 1,x                 ;  ( addr-t u-t cur ) drop through
0035A3r 3               
0035A3r 3               _entry_cmd_i:
0035A3r 3                               ; This is where i enters with a parameter that is calculated to
0035A3r 3                               ; be one before the current line, or given line, or so that we
0035A3r 3                               ; accept 0. We are ( addr-t u-t num )
0035A3r 3               
0035A3r 3               _cmd_a_have_para:
0035A3r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _num_to_addr        ;  ( addr-t u-t addr1 )
0035A6r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr xt_cr
0035A9r 3               
0035A9r 3               _next_string_loop:
0035A9r 3                               ; This is where we land when we are continuing in with another
0035A9r 3                               ; string after the first one. ( addr-t u-t addr1 )
0035A9r 3  20 rr rr                     jsr _get_input
0035ACr 3
